title: Mojo & Hylo Explained: The Future of High-Performance Coding

Mojo Programming Language:

Introduction to Mojo:

It is an upcoming programming language made by the company Modular. Mojo was created by Chris Lattner (creator of Swift and LLVM) and his team at Modular Inc. . The language is designed to combine Python's ease of use with C++-level performance, specifically targeting AI and high-performance computing workloads. The name "Mojo" is often accompanied by the fire emoji (🔥), and the language files can even use it as a file extension.

May 2, 2023—Modular announced Mojo and launched the Mojo Playground, a browser-based environment where developers could try Mojo through Jupyter notebooks.

September 2023 – Mojo became available locally on Linux systems.

October 2023—macOS support was added (including Apple Silicon).

March 2024—Mojo's standard library was open-sourced on GitHub under the Apache 2 license.

It is being considered the future and the most powerful programming language, as it is written the same as Python, and its execution speed is as fast as C++. This means its syntax is totally the same as Python's, but in terms of speed, it is C++. The company itself says that,

“Write like Python, Run Like C++”

Python Is Easy To Write:

Python is one of the easiest programming languages to write and understand, especially for beginners. The syntax is clean and readable, and the indentation rules are simple, making it more approachable than other programming languages.

Speed Problem in Python:-

The major problem with Python is its slow speed of execution. Python code is run by an interpreter, which reads and executes each line of code one at a time during runtime. Python is a lot slower than compiled languages because of the extra step of interpretation. This is especially true for CPU-heavy tasks.

Code Example of Python:

def add(x, y):
     return x + y

High Speed in C++:

C++ is among the fastest programming languages because it is a compiled language with manual memory management and zero-cost abstractions. C++ offers an excellent balance of speed and features. Its performance and hardware control make it the go-to choice for game engines, operating systems, browser engines (like Chrome's V8), and real-time applications.

C++ Is Difficult To Write:

C++ is often considered more difficult to learn and write than Python because of its complex syntax and requirement to understand lower-level concepts. For example, even a simple "add two numbers" function requires the following:

  • Including headers (#include <iostream>)
  • Defining types for each parameter (int add(int x, int y))
  • Writing a main() function to test it

This boilerplate code makes C++ less beginner-friendly and can slow down rapid development compared to Python's minimal syntax.

Code Example of C++:

#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
int add(int x, int y) {
     return x + y;
}

And that's the point where people choose Python to write the code and manage big projects of development and such, but when it comes to speed, people need to struggle with C++.

How Mojo Solves the Problem: -

The Mojo solves both of these problems by providing the speed of C++ and the clear, easy syntax of Python. It’s like two superpowers got together and made the most invincible programming language.

Syntax Example Of Mojo:-

def main():
       print("Hello, World!")

Advantages Of Mojo:-

Modern Programming Language:

Mojo unites the best of modern languages, including the usability of Python, the memory safety of Rust, and the powerful and intuitive compile-time metaprogramming of Zig. That means that Mojo is not just a hybrid of Python and C++, but a language that is carefully designed, taking the best from many modern programming languages.

Designed For AI:

Mojo is built for maximum performance across the full range of hardware used to run modern AI systems, from GPUs and TPUs to special accelerators. As a compiled, statically typed language, it is also great for agentic programming where reliability and performance at scale matter.

Pure Power:

No more compromises between power and productivity—Mojo is both. You can start with simple, familiar programming patterns, adding complexity when you need it. That is the future of coding.

Open source:

The Mojo standard library is fully open source on GitHub, and we welcome contributions! We also plan to open-source the Mojo compiler in 2026.

GPU programming:

Mojo makes GPU programming accessible to everyone, removing the need for vendor-specific libraries and separate compilation. Finally, you are able to write high-performance GPU kernels in the same language as for CPUs.

Python interop:

Mojo has native interop with Python, so you can cut out performance bottlenecks in existing code without rewriting everything. Start with one function, scale up as necessary, and use Mojo for performance-critical code. Your Mojo code is naturally importable into Python and packages together for distribution. You can also import libraries from the Python ecosystem into your Mojo code.

Conclusion:-

Python is easy but slow. C++ is fast but difficult. Mojo is one or the other. No more concessions. No more compromises. Simply, the wild performance with the soul of Python. Mojo is not the future of programming. That’s it now. And it’s on fire. 🔥

Hylo Programming Language:

Introduction to Hylo:

It is an experimental programming language made by a team of open-source developers. Hylo was created by Dave Abrahams (co-designer of Swift's core architecture) and Dmitri Gribenko, along with other language researchers. The language is designed to combine the safety and speed of Rust and C++ with a much simpler, easier-to-write coding model.

The name "Hylo" comes from the Greek word hyle, meaning "matter" or "substance," which reflects its focus on "values" rather than computer memory addresses.

2021–2022—The language started under the name Val as a research project to improve systems programming.

March 2023—The project was officially renamed to Hylo to avoid naming conflicts with other software.

Present — Hylo's compiler and standard library are completely open-sourced on GitHub, built mostly using the Swift language and the LLVM compiler toolchain.

It is being considered a major breakthrough in how we write fast software. It lets you write code that is as fast as C++ and as safe as Rust, but without the confusing rules (like Rust's borrow checker) that make those languages hard to learn. It achieves this by focusing completely on "values" instead of references.

The core idea behind Hylo is the following:

"Safe, fast, and simple systems programming."

Extreme safety in Rust:

Rust is designed with extreme safety in mind, enforced at compile time by the borrow checker. Each value has one owner. When the owner goes out of scope, the memory is automatically freed (no leaks, no double frees). The borrow checker also prevents use-after-free, dangling pointers, and data races (by allowing either a single mutable reference or many immutable references, never both). Buffer overflow can be caught at compile time by bounds checking. All this without a garbage collector. Giving C++-level performance with mandatory memory safety for Rust . If your Rust code compiles, it’s memory safe.

Rust has a brutal learning curve:

Rust's brutal learning curve comes from its borrow checker, which enforces strict ownership and lifetime rules at compile time. Simple tasks in other languages—like reusing a variable or building a linked list—become complex battles with the compiler. While Python lets you run broken code and C++ lets you shoot yourself in the foot, Rust forces discipline upfront. The result is safety, but the journey is steep and humbling.

Code Example of Rust: -

fn add(x: i32, y: i32) -> i32 {
     x + y
}

Swift is easy to read and write. -

Because its syntax is clean and expressive and reads like plain English. Swift removes the extra symbols you get in languages like C++ or Rust: no semicolons, no manual memory management, and no complex pointer syntax. Functions use natural keywords like "func," "return," and "->," and type inference allows you to write

"var name = 'Alice'" instead of "String name = 'Alice.'"

Swift also uses argument labels that make function calls self-documenting.

Swift is mostly locked into Apple's ecosystem:

Swift is tightly integrated with Apple’s ecosystem: iOS, macOS, watchOS, and tvOS. Apple open-sourced it for Linux and Windows, but most libraries (SwiftUI, UIKit, Core Data) and tools (Xcode) are Apple-only. There’s server-side Swift and cross-platform Swift, but with small communities. If you're writing Swift, you're locked in to Apple.

Code Example of Swift: -

func add(x: Int, y: Int) -> Int { x + y }

How Hylo Solves These Problems:

Hylo solves these problems by combining memory safety with simplicity, without a borrow checker or garbage collector.

Unlike C++, Hylo eliminates memory unsafety entirely by enforcing mutable value semantics—data is copied or moved by default, never shared, which prevents use-after-free, buffer overflows, and data races at compile time without requiring manual memory management.

Unlike Rust, Hylo removes the brutal learning curve by ditching the borrow checker altogether; instead, it uses compile-time lifetime elision that automatically infers when memory can be freed, so developers write natural, Swift-like code while still getting Rust-level safety.

Unlike Swift, Hylo is not locked into any ecosystem—it's designed for cross-platform systems programming—and it avoids ARC's runtime overhead by using deterministic, compile-time memory management. The result is a language that is as safe as Rust, as simple as Swift, as fast as C++, and as free as open source—without the compromises of any of them.

Syntax Example of Hylo:

fun add(x: Int, y: Int) -> Int { x + y }

Advantages of Hylo

Memory Safety

  • No use-after-free bugs
  • No buffer overflows
  • No data races
  • No dangling pointers
  • All memory safety guaranteed at compile time

No Borrow Checker

  • No lifetime annotations ('a, 'static)
  • No fighting the compiler over ownership rules
  • Gentler learning curve than Rust
  • Write naturally, like Python or Swift

No Garbage Collector

  • No runtime pause or slowdown
  • No ARC (Automatic Reference Counting) overhead
  • No retain cycles to debug
  • Deterministic, predictable performance

No Manual Memory Management

  • No malloc/free
  • No new/delete
  • No raw pointers to manage
  • No smart pointer complexity (shared_ptr, unique_ptr)

Cross-Platform

  • Not locked into any ecosystem (unlike Swift with Apple)
  • Works on Linux, Windows, macOS
  • Suitable for systems programming everywhere

Mutable Value Semantics

  • Data is copied or moved by default
  • No unexpected shared state
  • No accidental data races
  • Easier to reason about code

Performance

  • C++-level speed
  • Compile-time memory management
  • No runtime overhead

Simplicity

  • Clean, readable syntax
  • Type inference (write var x = 5, not Int x = 5)
  • No semicolons required
  • Swift-like ease with systems-level power

Conclusion:

Hylo is not just another programming language—it's a paradigm shift. For decades, developers have been forced to choose between safety (Rust and Swift), simplicity (Python and JavaScript), and performance (C++ and C). Hylo is the first language to deliver all three without compromise.

It eliminates C++'s memory vulnerabilities without forcing you to fight a borrow checker like Rust. It offers Swift-like readability without being locked into Apple's ecosystem or suffering ARC overhead. And it delivers Swift-like simplicity without sacrificing systems-level performance.

By combining mutable value semantics, compile-time memory management, and zero runtime overhead, Hylo solves the core tensions that have plagued systems programming for decades. The result is a language that is

  • Safer than C++
  • Simpler than Rust
  • More portable than Swift

faqs.question: Is Mojo better than Python?
faqs.answer: Mojo's most touted advantage over Python is that it is compiled ahead of time to machine-native code, using the LLVM toolchain. This gives Mojo programs an inherent speed advantage, although that advantage holds up best when working with features specific to Mojo.

faqs.question: What is the programming language of Mojo?
faqs.answer: Mojo is a systems programming language specifically designed for high-performance AI infrastructure and heterogeneous hardware. Its Pythonic syntax makes it easy for Python programmers to learn and it fully integrates the existing Python ecosystem, including its wealth of AI and machine-learning libraries.

faqs.question: Will Mojo replace C++?
faqs.answer: Mojo is unsure. As a reference, Apple is adding ownership in Swift, with the aim of fully replacing C++ in macOS and iOS. Mojo and Swift share some common ideals, after all, they have the same tech lead. To replace C++, you need to be perfectly compatible with C.

faqs.question: What language is the Mojo compiler written in?
faqs.answer: Mojo is an in-development proprietary programming language based on Python available for Linux and macOS. Mojo aims to combine the usability of a high-level programming language, specifically Python, with the performance of a system programming language such as C++, Rust, and Zig.

faqs.question: Is Mojo easy to learn?
faqs.answer: Mojo lets you start simple and gradually get powerful. By default, you can write “Pythonic” code, but when you need performance, you can switch into Rust-like explicit types and memory control. This runs orders of magnitude faster than the recursive version.

faqs.question: Is Mojo a low-level language?
faqs.answer: Positioned at the intersection of high-level programming simplicity and low-level performance optimization, Mojo demonstrates particular utility for artificial intelligence developers requiring both flexibility and computational efficiency.

faqs.question: Can Mojo run Python code?
faqs.answer: Calling Python from Mojo: You can import existing Python modules and use them in a Mojo program. This is 100% compatible because we use the CPython runtime without modification for full compatibility with existing Python libraries.

faqs.question: Is Mojo really fast?
faqs.answer: Mojo is really fast. But until it has Python's libraries, that speed is waiting for something to do. Fast code is nice. Actually being able to use it is better.

faqs.question: What can Mojo be used for?
faqs.answer: Mojo is built from the ground up to deliver the best performance on the diverse hardware that powers modern AI systems. As a compiled, statically-typed language, it's also ideal for agentic programming. No more choosing between productivity and performance - Mojo gives you both.

faqs.question: Is Mojo a serverside programming language or client side programming language?
faqs.answer: Mojo is a server-side and general-purpose programming language. It is primarily designed for high-performance backend tasks like machine learning, AI compute workloads, and cloud infrastructure rather than running in web browsers.

What Is Cloud Computing: -

Cloud computing is the on-demand delivery of computing resources (servers, storage, databases, networking, and software) over the internet on a pay-as-you-go basis.

Instead of buying and owning your own physical servers and data centers, you rent computing power from a cloud provider such as Amazon (AWS), Microsoft (Azure), or Google (GCP).

When it was introduced:

The concept of modern cloud computing was born in 2006 when Amazon launched AWS EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud), which allowed anyone in the world to rent virtual servers over the internet on a pay-as-you-go basis.

What was the main goal of introducing it: -

The primary goal of cloud computing is to eliminate the requirement for businesses to purchase and manage physical hardware. In the old days, companies had to drop big bucks on servers and storage, often throwing money away by buying more than they needed.

Cloud computing changes this by allowing companies only to pay for what they use, to scale up or down as needed, and to access computing power quickly. "Basically, it means computing can be as flexible and cheap as electricity. You pay for what you use, and you don't own the infrastructure

Who introduced it and why and where: -

Cloud computing was introduced by Amazon in 2006 with AWS EC2, utilizing their underused infrastructure to rent computing power to businesses. This innovation turned excess capacity into profit.

Before Amazon, Salesforce launched the Software as a Service (SaaS) model in 1999. However, Amazon's EC2 was the first true infrastructure as a service (IaaS), allowing users to rent virtual servers online. Consequently, Amazon is seen as the father of modern cloud computing, while Salesforce is recognized as the pioneer of cloud software.

Cloud Computing Characteristics:

On-Demand Self-Service:-

On-demand self-service is a fundamental aspect of cloud computing, allowing users to provision resources automatically without a provider assistance. Users can access a web console, utilize an API, or run command-line scripts to create virtual servers, allocate storage, set up databases, or configure networking—all independently and within minutes.

In contrast, traditional on-premises systems involved a slow process of requests, approvals, hardware orders, and delays for installation. Cloud computing eliminates these hurdles.

Whether you need one server or many, the cloud provides instant control. This self-service feature enhances agility—developers can quickly create test environments, businesses can launch products swiftly, and everyone can begin without waiting for others.

Broad Network Access:

Resources are available over the network and accessed through standard mechanisms that promote use by heterogeneous thin or thick client platforms (e.g., mobile phones, laptops, and workstations).

Unlike traditional systems that require physical connections, cloud services are accessed through web browsers, mobile apps, command-line tools, and APIs. This means developers can deploy servers from anywhere, salespeople can see customer info on the road, and engineers can monitor systems remotely.

Different operating systems are not a problem, thanks to cloud providers. Real-time data access makes remote work and global collaboration possible. Cloud computing also increases availability quite a lot. Without such wide availability, cloud computing would be limited to certain devices.

Resource Pooling:-

The cloud provider uses a multi-tenant model, where computing resources are pooled and shared among multiple customers. Rather than dedicating a physical server to a single customer, they pool computing power, storage, and networking from thousands of servers within huge data centers. Resources are allocated as needed, often without the customer knowing what specific physical machine they are using.

This method achieves economies of scale, allowing a single server to host numerous virtual machines from various companies, thereby lowering costs. Providers handle load balancing, fault tolerance, and resource allocation so customers only use what they need. Resource pooling is crucial for the cost-effectiveness of cloud computing, as it allows customers to share a large resource pool and efficiently meet demand.

Rapid Elasticity:-

Rapid elasticity, or scalability, is a key cloud feature that enables quick and often automatic adjustment of computing resources to match demand. During traffic spikes, such as flash sales or product launches, the cloud can swiftly add servers and increase bandwidth without manual effort. When traffic decreases, it automatically reduces resources, saving costs. Unlike traditional on-premises systems, which require advance planning and leave you with unused hardware, cloud elasticity allows for rapid scaling from one to thousands of servers.

This process is frequently automated through tools like auto-scaling groups that adjust resources based on metrics like CPU usage. Ultimately, this ensures optimal capacity management, providing sufficient power during peak times while minimizing waste during low traffic periods.

Measured Service:-

Measured service, or the pay-as-you-go model, allows customers to pay only for the cloud computing resources they actually use. Cloud providers monitor and report usage, tracking every action—such as server runtime, data storage, and API calls—resulting in precise billing. This contrasts with traditional IT, where companies pay upfront for hardware regardless of usage. With measured service, if you use a server for 3 hours, you pay for 3 hours.

This model applies to all service types: IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, and FaaS. Cloud providers offer clear dashboards and billing reports for cost transparency, enabling budget predictability. This shifts IT costs from capital expenditure (CapEx) to operational expenditure (OpEx), ensuring you only pay when resources provide value.

Cloud Deployment Models:-

Cloud deployment models specify the geographical location of cloud infrastructure, the management responsibilities, and access permissions. They encompass four main types, each tailored to different organizational needs and operational strategies, ensuring flexibility and control over cloud resources.

Public Cloud:-

Public cloud refers to a model where a third-party provider manages cloud infrastructure, making it available to the public online. Customers share the same physical resources in a multi-tenant architecture. Major providers include AWS, Microsoft Azure, GCP, Alibaba Cloud, and IBM Cloud, which handle everything from server procurement to security and maintenance.

Users can easily sign up and pay only for what they use, making it suitable for startups and businesses with fluctuating demands. While it offers significant scalability and cost-effectiveness, it also presents challenges like limited visibility into infrastructure and reliance on the provider's security measures. Overall, the public cloud is often the preferred option due to its scalability and global accessibility.

Private Cloud:-

Private cloud is a dedicated cloud infrastructure for a single organization, providing benefits like scalability, self-service, and resource pooling in an isolated environment. Unlike public clouds, which share resources among multiple users, private clouds are exclusively used by one organization, either on-premises or managed by a third party.

This model offers enhanced control, security, and compliance, allowing customization of networking, security policies, and hardware. It is favored in regulated industries such as government, finance, and healthcare. However, it requires significant investment in hardware and in-house expertise for management.

Essentially, a private cloud offers the advantages of cloud computing within the organization’s own infrastructure, but it demands a substantial budget and skilled team to maintain.

Hybrid Cloud:

Hybrid cloud integrates private and public clouds for efficient data sharing. It enhances security and compliance for sensitive workloads while providing the scalability and cost benefits of public clouds. For example, banks may keep customer data on private clouds for regulations but use public clouds during peak times.

This model is also useful for disaster recovery and real-time data replication. Common applications include developing and testing in public clouds before moving to private ones and performing big data analytics while keeping raw data secure on-premises. A strong network connection, often through VPNs or dedicated links, is essential for hybrid cloud operations, making it ideal for enterprises with substantial on-premises investments transitioning to public cloud solutions.

Multi-cloud:

Multi-cloud is a strategy where organizations utilize two or more public cloud providers simultaneously, distributing workloads to avoid reliance on a single vendor. This differs from hybrid cloud, which combines private and public clouds. Companies may run applications on AWS, store data on Google Cloud, leverage Azure for AI, and manage disaster recovery on IBM Cloud.

Key reasons for adopting multi-cloud include the following:

  • Avoiding vendor lock-in:

  • Spreading workloads allows for better pricing and flexibility to switch providers without major overhauls.

  • Best-of-breed services:

  • Organizations can select the best offerings from each provider, such as AWS Lambda for serverless computing and Google BigQuery for data warehousing.

  • Redundancy and resilience:

  • If one provider fails, workloads on others remain operational, enhancing business continuity.
    However, multi-cloud can be complex, requiring expertise across platforms, management of various billing and security systems, and effective networking tools to ensure seamless integration.
    This strategy is most common among large enterprises with the budget and technical staff to handle the complexity, while smaller organizations typically start with a single public cloud provider and evolve to multi-cloud only when specific business needs arise.

Cloud Computing Service Models: -

Cloud computing service models describe how providers allocate resources to customers, balancing management responsibilities between the customer and the provider. These models vary from high customer control to high provider management.

The primary aim is flexibility, catering to diverse customer needs. For example, large enterprises may seek complete control, while startups might prefer full provider management to concentrate on their core activities. Developers may only need to run simple span without server management.

Key service models include the following:

  • Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)

  • Platform as a Service (PaaS)

  • Software as a Service (SaaS)

  • Platform as a Service (PaaS)

Each model defines the responsibilities of the provider and the customer, influencing costs. Each model clarifies the roles of the provider and customer, affecting costs, scalability, security, and management. Choosing the right model is crucial to avoid unnecessary expenses or loss of control.

Infrastructure As A Service (IAAS):

Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) is a cloud computing model that provides on-demand access to virtualized resources such as servers, storage, and networking over the internet. The cloud provider manages the physical infrastructure, while customers control the operating systems and applications, offering significant flexibility and customization.

Notable IaaS solutions include Amazon EC2 for virtual servers and Amazon S3 for storage. Benefits include reduced hardware costs, instant scalability, and full control of the computing environment, though customers are responsible for updates, security, and backups.

IaaS is ideal for IT-savvy organizations needing cloud flexibility while maintaining control, similar to renting an apartment where the landlord provides the space, but you personalize it.

Platform As A Service (PAAS):

Platform as a Service (PaaS) is a cloud computing model that provides a complete environment for application development and deployment, managing everything from operating systems to databases. This enables developers to focus on their applications without the burden of infrastructure management.

Key benefits of PaaS:

  • Enhanced developer productivity (no server setup required)
  • Quick deployment (launch applications in minutes)
  • Automatic scalability (platform adjusts to traffic demands)
  • Lower operational costs (no dedicated infrastructure team needed)

However, PaaS has limitations: users are confined to the languages and tools offered by the provider and have less control compared to Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS). It is ideal for teams building web apps, mobile backends, APIs, and microservices, allowing them to focus on coding rather than infrastructure management.

In summary, PaaS is akin to renting a fully furnished apartment where the landlord handles everything—you simply provide your application span and start using it immediately.

Software As A Service (SAAS):

Software as a Service (SaaS) is a cloud model delivering software applications via the internet, managed by the provider. Customers access it through a web browser or mobile app without installation.

They pay a subscription fee based on usage or users and can start immediately. Examples include Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, and Salesforce.

Benefits of SaaS:

  • No infrastructure management
  • Automatic updates
  • Accessible from any internet-enabled device
  • Predictable pricing
  • Quick deployment

Trade-offs include limited customization, potential data security concerns, and reliance on internet connectivity. SaaS suits businesses needing standard applications like email and collaboration tools without software management hassles.

In summary, SaaS is like a hotel stay: ready to use without maintenance worries.

### Function As A Service (FAAS) / Serverless:-

Function as a Service (FaaS), or Serverless computing, is a cloud model enabling developers to execute span—functions—in response to events without managing servers. The cloud provider manages everything, including server setup, maintenance, and scaling. Developers write functions in languages like Python or Java, upload them, and set triggers like HTTP requests. Payment is based on actual compute time, not idle resources.

Key benefits of FaaS:
  • Cost efficiency: No charges for idle servers.
  • Automatic scalability: Functions run in parallel as needed.
  • Low operational overhead: No server management required.
  • Event-driven architecture: Suits modern applications.

Trade-offs include:
  • Cold start latency: Initial execution may be slower if the function hasn't run recently.
  • Execution time limits: Functions usually run for 5-15 minutes.
  • Statelessness: Functions can't retain data between executions.

FaaS is ideal for tasks like image processing and API backends, akin to ordering room service: you pay only for what you use.

Cloud Storage Types:-

Cloud storage types are methods for organizing and accessing data in the cloud, each designed for specific needs with trade-offs in performance, scalability, cost, and accessibility. Different data types require tailored storage solutions. Each type serves distinct functions, impacting your application's performance, reliability, and costs.

Direct Attached Storage: -

Direct Attached Storage (DAS) connects hard drives (HDDs) or solid-state drives (SSDs) directly to a single server or virtual machine, bypassing network use. In cloud computing, DAS serves as local storage for a virtual machine, referred to as an instance store or ephemeral storage, enabling rapid access and high input/output operations per second (IOPS).

However, DAS is limited to one virtual machine, restricting access from other servers. Data stored on DAS is often temporary; if the virtual machine is stopped or crashes, the data is lost. DAS is ideal for temporary tasks like caches, scratch data, and log files that can be recreated.

Cloud providers usually offer DAS as "instance store" volumes with specific instance types, typically at no extra cost. Think of DAS as a glove compartment in a car—it's easily accessible but loses everything if the car is irreparably damaged.

File Storage (NAS):

Network Attached Storage (NAS) provides hierarchical organization of data that can be accessed simultaneously by multiple users or servers over a network. NAS is a shared drive, unlike Direct Attached Storage (DAS), which is a single server. NAS uses protocols like NFS and SMB. It supports the file management operations like create, read, update, and delete and hence is suitable for a collaborative environment.  
Cloud file storage options provide scalable storage with redundancy. NAS: Think of a shared locker in an apartment. If you have permission, you can get to it. Slower than DAS because you have to go through the network.

Block Storage:-

Block storage breaks data into fixed-size pieces that virtual machines can access like a raw hard drive. Unlike file storage, which organizes data in folders, block storage looks like an empty disk to the operating system and needs a file system (like ext4 or NTFS). Each piece works on its own and can be spread across different physical devices, but the virtual machine sees them as one unit.

This setup provides high speed with low delay and quick input/output operations, allowing multiple disks to read and write at the same time. Block storage is great for databases, business applications, virtual machine boot disks, and tasks needing steady low-latency performance. In cloud environments, it often acts as persistent volumes that stay intact even when the virtual machine is off, allowing them to connect to other machines.

To put it simply, if DAS is like a glove compartment and file storage is a shared locker, block storage is like a piece of land you can build on, linked to your house (the virtual machine) by a fast private road. Access is private unless shared, and this land can be moved between houses if needed. Unlike file storage, which limits you to set shelves, block storage gives you a flexible solution for better performance.

Object Storage:-

Object storage manages data as individual units called objects, which consist of the data, metadata, and a unique identifier. Unlike traditional file storage, it employs a flat address space for better scalability. Objects are stored in buckets and accessed via API calls using unique keys, regardless of their physical location. This format is perfect for unstructured data like photos and videos, where entire objects are replaced instead of modified.

Benefits include unlimited scalability, high durability (up to 99.999999999%), built-in versioning, lifecycle management, and cost-effectiveness for infrequently accessed data.

Object storage resembles a warehouse of labeled boxes. You retrieve a box by its label, pay only for what you store, and the warehouse can expand without reorganization. However, it has higher latency than block storage and is unsuitable for databases or active transactions, making it ideal for modern cloud data needs.

Content Delivery Network (CDN):

A Content Delivery Network (CDN) is a network of servers located worldwide that stores copies of your content—like images, videos, and web pages—and delivers them to users from the nearest server. This reduces the time it takes for users to access content. For instance, a user in Tokyo can load an image much faster from a local CDN server than from a distant server in Virginia.

CDNs are optimized for quickly distributing static content and work best alongside an origin storage source. Benefits include faster content delivery, less strain on your main servers, protection against DDoS attacks, lower bandwidth costs, automatic failover, and an enhanced user experience.

Think of a CDN as placing vending machines with popular items in neighborhoods worldwide, allowing users to get what they want instantly. However, CDNs are ideal for static content and may have a slight delay when new content is first published as it spreads to all servers. CDNs are crucial for global applications, streaming services, e-commerce sites, gaming, and software distribution.

Architecture:-

Cloud computing architecture consists of various components that collaborate to provide cloud services. These components are categorized into three main areas: the front end (user interface), the back end (provider infrastructure), and the delivery network (connectivity).

Front End:-

The front end of cloud computing architecture is the user interface that enables clients to access cloud services through various applications. It includes two main types of clients:

  • Thin clients: Lightweight devices or web browsers that depend entirely on the cloud for processing and storage, allowing access from any device without software installation.
  • Fat clients: Fully featured applications installed locally that utilize cloud services for enhanced functionality, offering a better user experience with offline capabilities.
    Additionally, the front end encompasses user interface elements like dashboards and APIs, which allow users to manage resources, monitor usage, and control services efficiently.
    Users interact with the front end when uploading files, launching virtual servers, or generating reports. It serves as the interface that connects them to the back end, facilitating communication through requests and responses, bridging the gap between users and the underlying cloud infrastructure.

Back-end:

The back-end of cloud computing architecture is essential for supporting user interactions through the front end. It includes both physical and virtual resources, such as processing servers, data storage systems, and management software.

When a virtual machine is activated, it runs on a physical server in the provider's data center, with files stored in back-end storage arrays. These resources are organized into global clusters and data centers, acting as a unified pool. The back-end also incorporates hypervisors for virtualization, load balancers for traffic management, and platforms for authentication, billing, and monitoring.

It is standardized, automated, and designed to be fault-tolerant, ensuring seamless service continuity in case of component failures. While invisible to users, the back-end is crucial; without it, the front end lacks functionality.

Cloud-based Delivery & Network:-

Cloud-based delivery and networks are the infrastructure that connects users to cloud resources, facilitating data exchange. It operates over three main networks:

  • Internet: Offers global access to cloud services from any internet-enabled device, commonly used for public clouds.
  • Intranets: Private networks within organizations that ensure secure communication between on-premises systems and private or hybrid clouds without public exposure.
  • Intercloud: Connects various cloud providers, enabling seamless data and service movement across platforms like AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud for multi-cloud strategies and disaster recovery.

Key components include load balancers for traffic distribution, firewalls for security, and VPNs for encrypted connections.

Virtualization:-

Virtualization is the technology that creates virtual versions of physical computing resources such as servers, storage devices, networks, and operating systems, allowing multiple virtual instances to run on a single physical machine.

Virtual Machines:-

A Virtual Machine (VM) is a software simulation of a physical computer that operates its own operating system and applications, functioning like a real machine. It exists as a set of files on physical hardware managed by a hypervisor. Each VM believes it has its own CPU, memory, storage, and network interfaces, but these resources are actually shared from the physical server.

Multiple VMs can run on a single physical machine, isolated from one another—if one VM fails or gets infected, the others remain safe. VMs are essential in Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS), enabling quick provisioning of virtual servers without needing physical hardware.

Key benefits of VMs include:

  • Portability: Move between physical servers without downtime.
  • Snapshots: Save the exact state of a VM at any time.
  • Cloning: Create copies for testing or scaling.
  • Hardware Independence: VMs work regardless of the underlying hardware.

Common uses of VMs in the cloud involve running websites, databases, and applications that typically need a physical server. In essence, a virtual machine is a simulated computer that behaves like a real one, allowing multiple VMs to operate on a single physical machine.

Hypervisor:-

A hypervisor, or virtual machine monitor (VMM), is essential software that enables multiple virtual machines (VMs) to run on a single physical server. It manages resources like CPU, memory, and storage, ensuring each VM operates independently and securely.

There are two types of hypervisors:

  • Type 1 (bare-metal): Runs directly on hardware, offering superior performance and security, commonly used in data centers and cloud services.
  • Type 2: Operates on top of an existing operating system, suitable for personal use and development.

Popular examples include VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, and Oracle VirtualBox.

Without a hypervisor, virtualization wouldn't exist, allowing one server to host many VMs, which is fundamental to cloud computing. In essence, the hypervisor orchestrates the operation of VMs, ensuring they function smoothly without interference.

Bare Metal:-

Bare metal is a dedicated physical server for a single customer, providing direct access to all hardware resources like CPU, RAM, storage, and network interfaces without virtualization. Think of it as renting a whole house, while virtual machines are like apartments and containers are desks in a coworking space. Major cloud providers now offer bare metal servers for short-term use, enabling workload management without physical data center access.

Benefits of bare metal:

  • Maximum performance (no hypervisor overhead)
  • Predictable behavior (no virtualization issues)
  • Physical isolation for compliance (HIPAA, PCI-DSS, FedRAMP)
  • Full control over the OS and hardware
  • Simpler licensing based on physical cores

Trade-offs:
  • Slower provisioning (15-60 minutes vs. 2 minutes for VMs)
  • Less elasticity (adding capacity requires new servers)
  • Higher costs (paying for the entire server regardless of usage)
  • No snapshots or live migration (downtime during hardware failure)
  • Manual maintenance for OS updates and security
  • Manual OS updates and security maintenance

Bare metal is ideal for high-performance computing, gaming, heavy databases, compliance-focused industries, machine learning, and legacy applications. It’s less suitable for rapidly scaling workloads, small apps, short-term needs, development environments, or tight budgets.

Security:-

Cloud security is the collection of policies, technologies, and controls used to protect data, applications, and infrastructure in cloud computing environments from threats, breaches, and unauthorized access.

Data Encryption: -

Data encryption transforms readable data (plaintext) into an unreadable format (ciphertext) using algorithms and cryptographic keys. This process ensures that intercepted data remains unintelligible without the decryption key. It serves as a crucial defense in cloud security; if other protections fail and an attacker accesses your data, encryption renders it useless.

In cloud settings, encryption is applied in three states:

  • At rest: Data stored on disks and backups is encrypted automatically by cloud providers before being saved and decrypted when accessed, using keys you control.
  • In transit: Transport Layer Security (TLS) protocols protect data moving between your browser and cloud services, as well as between different cloud services.
  • In use: This involves data being processed in memory, which is less common and more complex.

Key management is vital—deciding who controls the encryption keys. You can allow the cloud provider to manage them (easier but less control), use customer-managed keys (more control but more responsibility), or bring your own keys (HYOK) stored in a hardware security module.

Without encryption, data in the cloud is exposed; anyone gaining access to the infrastructure can read it. Proper encryption safeguards your data even if physical media is stolen or lost, ensuring protection against unauthorized access.

Access Control:

Access control allows only verified users, applications, or services to access specific cloud resources based on their roles, following the principle of least privilege—granting only necessary permissions. This is managed through Identity and Access Management (IAM) systems, where user identities are created, assigned roles (like Admin, Developer, Viewer, or Billing), and linked to policies that define their allowed actions.

For example, a developer can create and delete virtual machines but cannot access production databases, while a database administrator can modify schemas but not change firewall rules. The key approach is to start users with no permissions and gradually add what they need, preventing unnecessary broad access.

In cloud environments, overly permissive IAM roles can lead to data exposure or unauthorized activities, such as cryptocurrency mining. Modern access control also considers factors like time, IP address, device compliance, and location, allowing access only during business hours from secure devices.

Regular reviews of access permissions are essential since roles can change, and permissions may remain if not revoked. Cloud providers offer tools like access analyzers to monitor permissions, anomaly detection for unusual usage, and just-in-time access requests for temporary elevated privileges. Poorly configured access control is a major cause of cloud data breaches, often due to simple errors like making storage buckets public or granting excessive permissions.

Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA):

Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) enhances security by requiring users to provide two or more verification factors to access cloud accounts. This method combines something you know (like a password), something you have (like a smartphone or security key), and something you are (like a fingerprint), making it much harder for attackers to breach accounts, even if they obtain your password.

Relying solely on passwords is risky—people often reuse them, fall for phishing scams, and choose weak options like "password123." With MFA, even if an attacker steals your password, they cannot log in without the second factor, such as a one-time span from an authenticator app or a fingerprint.

MFA blocks over 99.9% of account compromise attempts, yet many still avoid it due to the slight inconvenience of using their phone or a key. In cloud environments, MFA is essential for privileged accounts—root users and administrators who can access sensitive data.

Common MFA methods include:

  • Time-based one-time passwords (TOTP) from apps like Google Authenticator
  • Push notifications for login approvals
  • Hardware security keys like YubiKey
  • SMS text messages (less secure due to SIM swapping risks)
  • Biometrics like fingerprints or facial recognition

Most cloud providers enforce MFA for root accounts and encourage it for all users, often using adaptive MFA that prompts for additional factors only when a login appears risky.

Disabling MFA is a critical mistake; thinking a strong password is enough is misleading. Many recent data breaches could have been avoided with MFA enabled.

In summary, MFA strengthens account security by adding multiple layers of protection. Enable MFA on every cloud account, for every user, without exception.

Shared Responsibility Model:-

The shared responsibility model is one of the most important security concepts in cloud computing that accurately defines which security activities are conducted by the cloud provider and which are left to the customer. Having a clear understanding of this division is absolutely critical because the number one reason for cloud security breaches is misunderstanding this.

Provider's Responsibility (Security of the cloud):

The cloud provider ensures the security of the cloud infrastructure, managing everything unseen, such as data centers, hardware, networks, and essential software. They implement physical security measures like 24/7 guards, biometric access, and surveillance systems.

The provider oversees all hardware, including servers and storage, ensuring proper maintenance and secure disposal of old equipment. They also protect the virtualization layer, allowing multiple customers to run virtual machines on the same server without data access issues.

Additionally, the provider safeguards the network infrastructure, preventing unauthorized access and ensuring customer isolation. They guarantee availability and redundancy by distributing data centers across various regions and maintaining Service Level Agreements (SLAs) for uptime. Compliance with certifications like SOC 1, SOC 2, ISO 27001, PCI DSS, HIPAA, FedRAMP, and GDPR is also managed, benefiting customers' regulatory needs.

In essence, if it's not visible or manageable by you, the provider secures it, allowing you to focus on your applications and data. However, once you create a virtual machine or deploy an application, you enter your own responsibility zone.

Customer's Responsibility (Security in the cloud):

The customer is responsible for security in the cloud, covering everything they create, configure, upload, deploy, or manage. Many security failures occur because customers underestimate their responsibilities or mistakenly believe the provider will handle them.

Your responsibilities begin when you launch a virtual machine:

  • Choose a secure operating system image.
  • Configure it properly and apply security patches regularly.
  • Set up host-based firewalls and install antivirus software if necessary.
  • Disable unnecessary services and ports.
  • Manage user accounts and encrypt sensitive data on the VM's disks.

For containers, ensure secure image building, vulnerability scanning, and base image updates. For serverless functions, focus on writing secure span and managing third-party dependencies.

You are fully responsible for your data—encrypting it at rest and in transit, managing encryption keys, implementing backup and disaster recovery strategies, and preventing accidental exposure due to misconfigured permissions.

Access management is your duty:
  • Create user identities and groups.
  • Assign IAM roles and policies based on least privilege.
  • Enable multi-factor authentication for all accounts, especially administrative ones.
  • Regularly review and revoke unused permissions.
  • Monitor for suspicious login attempts.

Network security is also your responsibility:
  • Configure security groups, access control lists, route tables, and web application firewalls.
  • Ensure only necessary ports are open and trusted IP addresses can access sensitive resources.

You must monitor and log activities by enabling logging services, setting alerts for unusual activity, analyzing logs for signs of compromise, and investigating security incidents.

Finally, ensure compliance with regulations like GDPR, HIPAA, or PCI DSS by implementing required controls, conducting audits, and maintaining documentation.

The cloud provider offers tools to assist, but they won't do the work for you. Misconfigurations can lead to vulnerabilities, such as public storage buckets or overly permissive IAM roles. In the shared responsibility model, the provider secures the infrastructure, but you must secure your own environment. Many customers neglect this and face consequences.

Benefits:

1) Cost Benefits

1) No upfront hardware purchases

2) No wasted money on idle servers

3) Pay-as-you-go pricing (per hour, per GB, per request)

4) No data center electricity, cooling, or real estate costs

5) No IT staff needed for hardware maintenance

2) Scalability Benefits

1) Scale from 1 to 1,000 servers in minutes

2) Auto-scaling based on actual traffic

3) Global reach in seconds

4) Handle unexpected traffic spikes (viral events, flash sales)

3) Speed & Agility Benefits

1) Deploy resources in minutes (not weeks)

2) Experiment and fail fast with low cost

3) Test new ideas for pennies

4) Bring products to market faster

4) Reliability Benefits

1) Data replicated across multiple locations

2) Automatic failover if hardware dies

3) Built-in backup and disaster recovery

4) High uptime guarantees (99.9% to 99.999%)

5) Security Benefits

1) Enterprise-grade physical security

2) Data encryption at rest and in transit

3) DDoS protection built-in

4) Compliance certifications (SOC, ISO, PCI, HIPAA)

6) Global Reach Benefits

1) Deploy in regions worldwide

2) Serve customers with low latency

3) Content delivery networks (CDN) for fast global content

4) 24/7 availability from anywhere

7) Operational Benefits

1) No hardware to buy, rack, or cable

2) No patching physical servers

3) Automatic software updates

4) Simplified management through web console or API

8) Technologies Supported By Cloud

1) Artificial Intelligence (AI)

2) Machine Learning (ML)
3) Internet of Things (IoT)

4) Big Data Analytics

5) Blockchain

6) Quantum Computing

Most Popular Cloud Service Providers: -

2) Microsoft

FAQ.question: What is the definition of cloud computing?
FAQ.answer: Cloud computing is the on-demand availability of computer resources and services over the internet. Instead of having to build and maintain your own physical data centers or hard drives, you simply rent access to these capabilities from companies like Amazon Web Services or Microsoft Azure, and pay only for what you use.

FAQ.question: What are the 4 types of cloud computing?
FAQ.answer: There are four main services within these deployment models: infrastructure as a service (IaaS), platform as a service (PaaS), software as a service (SaaS), and serverless computing.

FAQ.question: What are the 4 pillars of cloud computing?
FAQ.answer: What Are the 4 Pillars of Cloud Computing? Understanding We discuss the 4 pillars of cloud computing, four core areas: Strategy & Architecture, Integration & Migration, Application Development, and Modernization & Governance.

FAQ.question: What are the top 3 cloud computing companies?
FAQ.answer: The CCSE credential covers cloud security topics for all of the top three cloud providers: Amazon Web Services (AWS), Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud Platform (GCP). In addition, the CCSE covers key security concepts such as risk management, identity and access management, data encryption and incident response.

FAQ.question: How is AI used in cloud computing?
FAQ.answer: AI is a key piece of cloud computing because it enhances the automation, decision-making, and flexibility of cloud services with the ability to scale compute and storage independently. Cloud computing provides the infrastructure for AI and allows organizations to leverage AI without major investment.

FAQ.question: What are the 4 C's of cloud security?
FAQ.answer: Integrate security at every cloud layer — from cloud infrastructure to code — by applying the 4Cs (Cloud, Cluster, Container, Code) framework for resilient cloud-native applications.

FAQ.question: What is the biggest cloud company in the world?
FAQ.answer: Who is the biggest cloud provider in 2026? By 2026, Amazon Web Services (AWS) is the world’s leader in cloud infrastructure, with about 31% of the market share. Microsoft Azure has 25% and Google Cloud Platform has 11%.

FAQ.question: What are the risks of using cloud services?
FAQ.answer: 7 Big Risks of Cloud Computing Are No Visibility, Misconfigurations in the Cloud, loss of data, Accidental data disclosure, Identity Theft, API and Insecure Integration And Sovereignty of Data.

FAQ.question: Can I run any OS on an IaaS server?
FAQ.answer: Custom environments: You have root access to your instances, allowing you to install any OS, runtime, or software package you need.

FAQ.question: What are three types of cloud storage?
FAQ.answer: There are three main cloud storage types: object storage, file storage, and block storage.

title: History Of Programming Languages

Introduction:

Definition:

Aprogramming language is a formal, structured system of communication that allowsa human (programmer) towrite instructions (code) that a computer can execute. Programming languages arelogical andunambiguous.Human languages are full of ambiguity (jokes, sarcasm, double meanings).

Explanation With Example:

The purpose of a programming language can be divided into three parts:

  1. Abstraction: It hides the complicated electricaldetails of the computer (voltage, transistors, binary) behind simplewords like PRINT orADD.
  2. Precision: It makes the human being 100% logical. InEnglish you can say "get the file" (which file? where?). In aprogramming language, you haveto say exactly "OPEN file 'data.txt.'"
  3. Automation: You write one time, and the instructionsare executed millions of times without getting tired.

Example:-

The Task:Tell the computer tosay "HI."

Code: print("HI")

What goes on inside the computer:

Step

What the computer looks at

What it means

1

print()

Prepare to print something on the screen.

2

HI

The particular thing to output is the letters H and I

3

Screen: HI

"Finished."

Reason To Introduce (ProgrammingLanguages):

The general reason: -

Programming languages were introduced to save time, reduce errors, and allowhumans to communicate with computers without needing a degree in electricalengineering.

The Core Problem: "The Machine LanguageTrap"

Before there were programming languages, programmers had to program in machinecode (binary).

What that looked like:

Text10110000 0110000110110010 0000000111000100 00000001

The problem with this:

Problem:

Explanation:

Too slow.

10 seconds to write one line of binary. A real program willrequire1,000,000 lines.

Too prone to errors

One wrong 0 instead of a 1 crashes everything.

Too hard to read.

Come back to your code after one week. Can you still read10110010?No.

Too narrow

Code written for an Intel CPU won't run on an ARM CPU (like aphone).

The solution: invent a programming language that looks likeEnglish. Then let a compiler (translation program) automatically convert it tobinary.

Whofirst discovered this concept?

Ada Lovelace (1815-1852), a British mathematician and daughter of Lord Byron, iscelebrated as “The First Programmer” for writing the first computer program in1843. She workedwith Charles Babbage on the Analytical Engine, creating an algorithm forBernoulli numbers andintroducingkey programming concepts like variables and loops.

Lovelace famously stated that "the Analytical Engine weaves algebraic patterns,just as the Jacquard loom weaves flowers," highlighting the link between loomsand computers. WhileBabbage focused on hardware, Lovelace's vision of software paved the way forprogramming. Her 1843 codemirrors modern programming logic.

Though many credit Babbage or Alan Turing as the first programmers, it was Lovelacewho established foundational programming concepts in 1843, ahead of KonradZuse's development ofcontemporary programming languages in the 1940s.

Highlights:

  • Who: Ada Lovelace
  • Date: 1843.
  • Discovery: Step-by-step instructions for machines (variables,operations, loops)
  • Machine: Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine
  • Importance: The first algorithm for a machine

Lovelace's mother advised her against writing poetry and encouraged her to focuson math to steer clear of her father's "madness." Interestingly, this choice ledto hersignificant contributions, including concepts like variables, operations, andloops.

Programming Languages Introduced Between 1800 -1850:-

AnalyticalEngine Algorithm:-

The Analytical Engine Algorithm, created by Ada Lovelace in 1843 for CharlesBabbage's proposed Analytical Engine, is celebrated as the first computerprogram. AlthoughLovelace's algorithm was never executed on a machine, it aimed to computeBernoulli numbers. Sheinnovatively utilized operation cards for calculations and variable cards formemory, effectively layingthegroundwork for early programming syntax.

Lovelace introduced fundamental programming concepts, including variables,operations, and loops. She likened the Analytical Engine to a Jacquard loom,illustrating how machinescouldmanipulate symbols similarly to how looms use punched cards to produce intricatepatterns.

The Analytical Engine Algorithm (1843) holds historical importance as itdemonstrated a general-purpose machine's capability to perform symbolicinstructions for solvingmathematical problems. This pioneering work established a crucial foundation forfuture programminglanguages and marked a significant shift from simple calculations to the realmof programmablecomputing.

Programming Languages Introduced Between 1850 -1900:-

The Tide-Predicting Machine (1872-1876):

The Tide-Predicting Machine, developed by Sir William Thomson (Lord Kelvin)between 1872 and 1876, was an early analog mechanical computer designed toforecast ocean tides. Itoperatedwithout programming languages, using pulleys, gears, and specially shaped camsto represent mathematicalformulas related to tidal movements.

To configure the machine for a specific port, an operator would install a set ofcams corresponding to various tidal influences, such as the sun's and moon'sgravitational effects.By turning a crank, the machine generated a continuous graph of predicted tidelevels.

While lacking a symbolic programming language, the Tide-Predicting Machine wasinnovative in showing that machines could be reconfigured to solve differentproblems, paving the wayforfuture punched-card programming.

In summary, the Tide-Predicting Machine was a groundbreaking analog computerthat utilized physical cams to predict tides, illustrating the adaptability ofmachines for variouscalculations long before digital programming emerged.

HermanHollerith's Tabulating Machine (1884-1890):

Herman Hollerith's Tabulating Machine, created between1884 and 1890, was a groundbreaking electromechanical device for processing dataduring the 1890 U.S.Census. It revolutionized data handling by using punched cards, where each cardrepresented anindividual oritem, with holes indicating details like age, gender, occupation, andhometown.

The machine was "programmed" through a plugboard, which connected wires tospecify which holes activated counting mechanisms. This innovative approach isrecognized as the firstinstance of physical programming, allowing different wiring configurations tocount various data types.Forinstance, one setup could tally all males, another could count farmers, and athird could trackunmarriedwomen over 30.

Remarkably, the Tabulating Machine could process about 80 cards per minute, slashingthe census completion time from eight years to just one. Hollerith's punchedcard system laid thegroundwork for future programming languages, as punched cards remained thestandard input method forcomputers until the 1970s. His company later merged to form IBM (InternationalBusiness MachinesCorporation) in 1924.

In summary, Hollerith's Tabulating Machine introduced punched cards andplugboard programming, demonstrating that machines could be reconfigured fordifferent tasks withouthardware changes, ultimately shaping the future of data processing and leadingto the establishment ofIBM.

Programming Languages Introduced Between 1900 -1950:

Plankalkül (1940s):

Plankalkül, meaning “Plan Calculus,” was the firsthigh-level programming language, developed by German pioneer Konrad Zuse between1943 and 1945. Unlikeprevious programming techniques that relied on physical elements, Plankalkül wasa symbolic languagedesigned for Zuse's Z3 and Z4 computers. Unfortunately, it was never implementedduring Zuse'slifetime.

This innovative language utilized a unique two-dimensionalnotation system, where variables were represented by letters withsubscript indices(e.g., V₀, V₁, A₂), and operations were neatly organized. Plankalkül introducedseveral advanced concepts, including the following:

  • Assignment (saving values in variables)
  • Conditional statements (if-then-else logic)
  • Loops (instructions that repeat)
  • Subroutines (reusable code snippets)
  • Floating point math (decimal numbers)

Zuse used Plankalkül to develop chess algorithms and sortingalgorithms. However, it remained largely unknown until 1972, long after theemergence of popularlanguageslike FORTRAN and COBOL. In summary, Plankalkül was a groundbreaking languagethat laid the foundationfor many programming concepts we use today, yet it went unrecognized for nearly30 years.

Assembly Language (Late 1940s - Early1950s):

Assembly language was the first programminglanguageto use human-readable symbols, known asmnemonics,instead of raw binary or machine code, emerging in the late1940s and early 1950s. Prior to Assembly, programmers wrote instructions as longstrings of 1s and 0s,whichwas slow and prone to errors.

Assembly Language introducedshort, memorablemnemonicslike MOV (move data), ADD (add numbers), SUB (subtract),JMP (jump),and CMP (compare). Each assembly instruction directly corresponds to a machinecode instruction, makingit alow-level language closely tied to hardware.

To convert assembly code into machine code, an assembler program was used. The firstpractical assemblers were developed in the late 1940s for machines like theEDSAC at CambridgeUniversity.

Assembly language provided several advantages over binary:

  • Readability: Easier for humans tounderstand.
  • Writability: Reduced likelihood oftypos.
  • Memory: Efficiency: No wasted space, aseach instruction directly mapped to one machine instruction.

However, it was still machine-specific, meaning code for one CPU would not run onanother.

In summary, Assembly Language (late 1940s-early 1950s) introduced mnemonics likeMOV, ADD, and JMP, requiring an assembler for translation, and became the firstwidely usableprogramminglanguage, despite its machine-specific nature.

ShortCode (1949-1950):

Short Code, also known as the Short Order Code, was one of the firsthigh-level programming languages implemented on a working computer. Here are thekey points:

  • Developers: John Mauchly and WilliamSchmitt
  • Years: 1949-1950
  • Target Computer: UNIVAC I

Key Features:

  • High-Level Language: Allowed for morehuman-friendly syntax compared to assembly language.
  • X = Y + Zinstead of multipleassembly instructions.
  • Mathematical Expressions: Programmers couldwrite expressions like the following:
  • Translated code line-by-line during execution.
  • Slower performance: 10 to 50 times slower than hand-writtenassembly code.
  • Interpreted Language:
  • Used letters (e.g., X, Y, Z) for variables.
  • Variable Representation:
  • Single characters for operations:
  • 01= add
  • 02= subtract
  • 03= multiply
  • 04= divide
  • Operation Representation:
  • Resembled a coded message rather than English.
  • Program Appearance:

Historical Significance:

  • Proved that a high-level language could function on realhardware.
  • Paved the way for future languages like FORTRAN.

Summary:

  • Seamless calling of Python (via PyCall), C, and Fortranlibraries

  • Applications of Julia:

  • Scientific research (astronomy, climate modeling, genomics)
  • Machine learning (Flux.jl, Knet)
  • Quantitative finance

  • Notable users:

  • MIT
  • NASA
  • Federal Reserve Bank of New York

  • In summary, Julia combines thefollowing:

  • Mathematical expressiveness
  • Raw performance
  • The best of Python and C

Swift(2014):

  • Swift is a modern, fast, and safe programming language by Apple,replacing Objective-C.

  • It is easy to learn, safe by default,and highly performant for the following:

  • iOS applications
  • macOS applications
  • watchOS applications
  • tvOS applications
  • Swift syntax is clean and expressive, resembling plain English, andeliminates many quirks of Objective-C.

  • Key features include:

  • Optionals: Handle the absence of values,preventing null pointer crashes.
  • Type Inference: The compilerdetermines types automatically.
  • Guard Statements: Allow early exit fromfunctions.
  • Closures: Self-contained blocks offunctionality.
  • Extensions: Add functionality to existingtypes.
  • Protocols: Define method blueprints.
  • Automatic Reference Counting (ARC):Apple's memory management system.
  • Swift is open-source and compatible with Linux, enablingserver-side development with frameworks like Vapor and Perfect.
  • It is one of the fastest-growing programming languages and rankshigh in developer satisfaction.

  • Major apps built with Swiftinclude:

  • Lyft
  • Airbnb
  • WhatsApp
  • All Apple apps
  • In summary, Swift is a modern,safe, and enjoyable language for developing within the Apple ecosystem,now expanding toserver-sideapplications.

Ballerina (2017):

  • Ballerina is a modern programming language for cloud-nativeapplications, microservices, and APIs.

  • It offers built-in support for:

  • Network communication
  • JSON, XML, HTTP, GraphQL
  • Database access
  • Ballerina is designed as a "cloud-native programminglanguage."

  • Code visualization:

  • Ballerina code can be represented as sequence diagrams, showing theflow of network calls visually.

  • Key features:

  • Network-aware types (clients, listeners, services as first-classcitizens)
  • Built-in support for distributed transactions (compensatingtransactions)
  • Flexible type system (union types, optional types, openrecords)
  • Concurrency based on sequence diagrams (workers function likethreads)
  • JSON as a native data type (no need for parsing libraries)
  • Seamless integration with Docker and Kubernetes

  • Use cases:

  • API gateways
  • Cloud integration platforms
  • Event-driven architectures
  • Microservice orchestration
  • Companies using Ballerina include WSO2 and various enterpriseintegration projects.
  • In summary, Ballerina is thefirst programming language built from the ground up for the cloud, APIs,and microservices,makingnetwork programming as intuitive as arithmetic.

Zig (2020):

  • Zig is a modern systems programming language that serves as asimpler, safer alternative to C.

  • It features:

  • No hidden allocations
  • No macros
  • No operator overloading
  • No exceptions
  • This design ensures completely predictable code behavior.

  • Zig is built on four mainpillars:

  • Simplicity: The language is small.
  • Performance: Comparable speed to C.
  • Safety: Includes compile-time checks andoptional runtime safety checks.
  • Interoperability: Can directly call C code,and vice versa.

  • Key features include:

  • Compile-time code execution: Functions canrun at compile time, enabling powerful generics without macros.
  • No hidden control flow: Avoids exceptions,operator overloading, and RAII.
  • Error handling as values: Errors arereturned instead of thrown.
  • Explicit allocators: You control memoryallocation without hidden allocations.
  • Defer statements: Code runs when leavingscope, similar to Go's defer.
  • Cross-compilation: Target any platform fromany platform seamlessly.

  • Applications of Zig:

  • Game development
  • Compilers
  • Embedded systems
  • Performance-critical infrastructure
  • Zig is rapidly gaining popularity as a C replacement, offeringmodern conveniences without complex features like Rust's borrowchecker.
  • In summary, Zig is a simple,predictable, and powerful successor to C, providing full control with nohidden costs.

Carbon(2022):

  • Carbon is an experimental programming language by Google, proposedas a successor to C++.
  • Its goal is to serve as a "C++ replacement" with modernlanguage features while ensuring seamless interoperability with existingC++ code.
  • Unlike Rust, which has a different memory model, Carbon is designedto integrate within C++ codebases, allowing calls between Carbon and C++in the samefile.
  • This design enables large existing C++ codebases, such asGoogle's extensive code, to gradually transition to Carbon withoutneeding a completerewrite.
  • Modern generics, akin to Rust traits or C++ concepts
  • Memory safety enhancements, including default bounds checking andnull safety
  • A simple and consistent syntax, eliminating legacy C quirks
  • Modular code organization using importinstead of#include
  • Bidirectional interoperability with C++
  • Carbon is still in its early stages and not yet ready forproduction, but it indicates a potential direction for systemsprogramming.
  • In summary, Carbon representsGoogle's vision for the future of systems programming: a modern languagethat can incrementallyreplace C++, unlike Rust, which necessitates complete rewrites.

FAQ.question: Does NASA use C++ or Python?
FAQ.answer: NASA uses both C++ and Python, but for entirely different purposes. They are utilized together based on distinct software requirement.

FAQ.question: Which is the no. 1 coding language?
FAQ.answer: The 'number one' coding language depends on how you measure it—whether by general popularity, usage among professional developers, or industry demand. As of 2025 and 2026, the titles are generally split between Python and JavaScript.

FAQ.question: Which coding language does Elon Musk know?
FAQ.answer: Elon Musk has stated that traditional programming languages will soon become obsolete as artificial intelligence begins to generate optimized binary code directly. Historically, he is self-taught and proficient in several languages, utilizing Python, C, C++, and Java across his companies like Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI.

FAQ.question: Is AI better at Python or C++?
FAQ.answer: In the AI field, Python and C++ are not usually rivals but partners. Python is the primary language for high-level development, experimentation, and research. C++ is the workhorse for performance-critical backends, infrastructure, and real-time deployment.

FAQ.question: Which country is no. 1 in coding?
FAQ.answer: There is no single 'no. 1' country, as rankings depend on whether you measure by overall test scores, talent pool volume, or industry impact. However, the top spots are consistently held by China, Russia, and Poland in skills-based coding challenges.

FAQ.question: Is coding harder than math?
FAQ.answer: Whether coding is harder than math depends entirely on what type of coding and math you are doing, but generally, coding is more approachable, while math is more conceptually demanding.

FAQ.question: What programming language did Bill Gates use?
FAQ.answer: Bill Gates's primary programming language—and the one that launched Microsoft—is BASIC. Alongside Paul Allen, he wrote Altair BASIC in 1975 using Intel 8080 Assembly language.

FAQ.question: Is ChatGPT written in Python?
FAQ.answer: Yes, ChatGPT is primarily built using Python. The core of the AI is trained, fine-tuned, and run using Python, which relies heavily on machine learning libraries like PyTorch.
